blocks.content: They ate on the back porch, the shaded side, where the box fan moved the air enough to make the afternoon bearable.
Mrs. Brooks sat with them for a while, asking the questions she always asked — how was the summer going, had they been to the pool.

blocks.speaker: Eli
blocks.content: Not the pool.

blocks.speaker: Mrs. Brooks
blocks.content: You should go to the pool.

blocks.speaker: Eli
blocks.content: The pool's real crowded.

blocks.speaker: Mrs. Brooks
blocks.content: That's because it's summer.

blocks.speaker: Eli
blocks.content: Yeah. That's the problem.
She gave him the look. He accepted it.
Daisy sat beside Kit, close enough that their elbows were almost touching. She'd eaten half a sandwich and was picking at the second half.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: What.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: Nothing.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: You're thinking.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: People think. That's allowed.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: You do the thing with your sandwich when you're thinking.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: I do not do a thing.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: You pull the crusts apart.
She looked at the plate. Four separate crust pieces, arranged around the edges.
She reassembled the sandwich with a certain dignity.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: Maybe I like the crusts separate.
Mrs. Brooks, from her end of the porch, said nothing. But her shoulders moved slightly.
